content: Connectives are linking words. They
join ideas together. Two types:
CONJUNCTIONS: the short ones.
They join within a sentence:
and, but, so, yet, or, because,
although, while, unless, if
CONJUNCTIVE ADVERBS: the formal
ones. They link between sentences
and need a full stop or semicolon:
however, therefore, nevertheless,
furthermore, consequently,
meanwhile, otherwise, moreover
'But' and 'however' mean the same
thing. The difference is where they
sit and what comes before them.
CONTRAST:
I was tired, but I came.
I was tired. However, I came.
Same idea, different formality.
CAUSE:
I left because it was late.
It was late, so I left.
It was late. Therefore, I left.
CONCESSION:
Although it hurt, I kept going.
It hurt. Nevertheless, I kept going.
CONDITION:
If it rains, we stay home.
Unless it stops, we stay home.
Same meaning, opposite framing.
There is not too much to know here.
A paragraph is one complete thought
in detail. When the thought changes,
start a new paragraph.
Opening and closing sentences often
reference the paragraphs beside
them. This is what makes writing
flow from one idea to the next.
One important rule: 'it' must be
re-established at the start of a
new paragraph. The reader needs to
know what 'it' refers to again.
Short paragraphs are easier to read.
When in doubt, start a new one.
